food_name: Kellogg's Corn Flakes (30g serving, dry, without milk)

local_names.romanized_local: Jiā Lè Shì Yù Mǐ Piàn

Jiā Lè Shì Yù Mǐ Piàn

chinese: 家乐氏玉米片 (Jiā Lè Shì Yù Mǐ Piàn)
romanized: Jiā Lè Shì Yù Mǐ Piàn

region_of_origin: United States (invented 1894, Battle Creek, Michigan); Singapore market formulation by Kellogg (Australia) Pty Ltd
food_subgroup: Ready-to-eat breakfast cereal made from milled corn (maize), sugar, salt, and malt flavouring, baked; B-vitamin and iron fortified

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn covers generic corn flakes cereal (FOODON_03301710). Brand-specific Kellogg's Singapore entry is not available; closest_parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Corn flakes — ready-to-eat breakfast cereal, closest FoodEx2 parent for processed corn-based cereal
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not carry a brand-specific Kellogg's Corn Flakes code; A000F (processed cereal products) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept maps precisely to branded corn flakes cereal; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Kellogg's Corn Flakes is 30g dry (approximately 1.5 cups of flakes), providing approximately 111 kcal before any milk is added.

nutrient_source.title: Kellogg's Corn Flakes — Singapore Nutrition Information Panel (NIP); cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Cereals ready-to-eat, KELLOGG'S CORN FLAKES (FDC ID 173263)
nutrient_source.organization: Kellogg (Australia) Pty Ltd / Kellogg Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from the Kellogg's Singapore product NIP (verified label data). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Kellogg's Corn Flakes (FDC ID 173263) used as cross-reference to confirm macro and micronutrient plausibility. Singapore NIP values take precedence where they differ from USDA due to regional fortification differences.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — Kellogg's Corn Flakes (GI = 81, glucose reference); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI = 81 (HIGH) is a well-established published value for Kellogg's Corn Flakes from the University of Sydney GI Database and multiple peer-reviewed publications (Jenkins et al.; Atkinson et al. Diabetes Care 2008). Glycemic load per 30g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (81 × 25.2) / 100 = 20.4 (HIGH). Available carbohydrate per 30g = 84 × 0.30 = 25.2g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 500mg per 100g (150mg per 30g serving); moderate for a dry breakfast cereal
hypertension.guidance: A standard 30g dry serving contributes approximately 150mg sodium (6% of the 2,400mg daily limit); hypertensive patients should monitor total sodium intake across the full meal, particularly if full-cream milk, salted butter, or additional toppings are added, and should prefer low-sodium cereal alternatives where sodium restriction is clinically indicated.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (GI = 81) and high glycemic load per serving (GL = 20.4); one of the most glycemically harmful common breakfast foods despite its perceived 'healthy' image
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kellogg's Corn Flakes carry a GI of 81 — higher than white bread (GI 71) and white rice (GI 64) — making them a high-priority counselling target for T2DM patients in Singapore who commonly consume corn flakes as a 'safe' breakfast; clinicians should advise replacement with low-GI alternatives such as rolled oats (GI ~55) or high-fibre bran cereals, and should explicitly address the health-halo misconception driven by low fat content and fortification marketing.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.2g/100g) and zero trans fat are favourable; however, high refined carbohydrate content (84g/100g) and high GI drive postprandial triglyceride elevation and insulin resistance, which are independent CVD risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the saturated fat and cholesterol profile of corn flakes is benign for cardiovascular risk, the high glycemic load (GL 20.4 per 30g serving) promotes postprandial hyperglycaemia and hypertriglyceridaemia; CVD patients and those with metabolic syndrome should limit portion size and combine with a protein source (e.g., low-fat milk or Greek yogurt) to attenuate the glycemic response, or switch to a lower-GI whole-grain cereal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High GI promotes insulin spikes and pro-inflammatory cytokine release; minor benefit from vitamin D fortification (2.5mcg/100g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Regular consumption of high-GI breakfast foods such as corn flakes is associated with increased CRP in observational studies; RA patients should be counselled to replace corn flakes with anti-inflammatory low-GI alternatives, while noting that the vitamin D fortification (0.75mcg per 30g serving) provides only a minor contribution to the recommended 15mcg/day intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kellogg's Corn Flakes is a ready-to-eat breakfast cereal made from milled corn (maize), sugar, salt, and malt flavouring, baked into thin crisp flakes and fortified with iron, B vitamins (thiamin, riboflavin, niacin, B6, folate, B12), and vitamin D. The Singapore market formulation is produced by Kellogg (Australia) Pty Ltd and is the most widely consumed breakfast cereal across all demographic groups in Singapore. Per 100g, the cereal provides 370 kcal, 7g protein, 1g fat, and 84g carbohydrate with 9g sugar and 3g dietary fibre. A standard 30g dry serving (approximately 1.5 cups) provides approximately 111 kcal. The texture is light and crisp, with a mild sweet corn flavour; the flakes soften quickly in milk. From a clinical standpoint, Kellogg's Corn Flakes represent one of the most important patient misconception foods in Singapore: the GI of 81 is higher than white bread (GI 71) and white rice (GI 64), making it one of the most glycemically harmful common breakfast foods. The low fat content (1g/100g), the fortification with B vitamins and iron, the Kellogg's brand marketing ('start your day right'), and the association with wholesome breakfast rituals create a powerful health halo that is entirely misleading for glycemic health. The glycemic load per standard 30g serving is 20.4 (HIGH). Singapore T2DM patients who eat corn flakes every morning are starting their day with one of the most potent blood sugar spikes available from a mainstream food. Replacing corn flakes with rolled oats is one of the single most impactful breakfast changes a T2DM patient can make. The sodium content of 500mg/100g (150mg per 30g serving) is moderate for a dry cereal. The iron fortification (8mg/100g) is clinically significant for iron-deficiency prevention. Vitamin D fortification (2.5mcg/100g) provides a minor contribution to daily requirements.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the Kellogg's Singapore product NIP (verified label data, confidence 1.0 per input), cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Kellogg's Corn Flakes (FDC ID 173263). GI value of 81 is a well-established published figure from the University of Sydney GI Database with multiple peer-reviewed confirmations. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully populated from the verified product label.
notes: trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per Kellogg's Singapore NIP — product formulation contains no trans fats. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per Kellogg's Singapore NIP — plant-based cereal product contains no cholesterol. vitamin_c_mg is confirmed zero per Kellogg's Singapore NIP — no vitamin C fortification in the Singapore formulation. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is confirmed zero per Kellogg's Singapore NIP — no vitamin A fortification in the Singapore formulation. vitamin_e_mg is confirmed zero per Kellogg's Singapore NIP — no vitamin E fortification in the Singapore formulation. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported on the Kellogg's Singapore NIP and not available in the USDA SR Legacy entry for this product at the level of individual fatty acid fractions. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported on the Singapore NIP; USDA SR Legacy reports approximately 2.9g water per 100g for Kellogg's Corn Flakes but this value is not confirmed on the Singapore label and is therefore excluded to preserve source integrity. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per 30g serving) / 100 = (81 × 25.2) / 100 = 20.4, where available carbohydrate per 30g = 84g/100g × 30g = 25.2g. The malt flavouring in Kellogg's Corn Flakes is barley-derived and contains gluten; the product is NOT suitable for coeliac disease or gluten intolerance despite being a corn-based cereal. Halal status confirmed per Kellogg's Singapore product labelling. The Singapore formulation includes mandatory B-vitamin and iron fortification consistent with Singapore food regulations.
